I'd play Qh7. Your move h4 pushed a pawn while leaving your own pawn on h3 loose and doing nothing active with your strongest piece — the queen. Instead, put the queen on h7 where it does three useful things at once: it eyes Black's pawns on f7 and g6, and it keeps h3 protected. When your queen is active and defending at the same time, you keep the initiative instead of drifting.

Takeaway: In the endgame, use your queen actively — one good queen move can attack and defend at the same time, which a quiet pawn push can't.

I'd play Qh7. The problem with h4 is that it's a passive pawn push that ignores your best asset — your active queen — and it lets Black's king wander up to help while your h3-pawn stays a target. With Qh7 you attack both the f7 and g6 pawns while still keeping h3 defended, so your queen is doing three useful jobs at once instead of shuffling a pawn. Get your most powerful piece working before you touch quiet pawn moves.

Takeaway: In sharp queen-and-pawn positions, activate your queen to attack and defend at the same time rather than making passive pawn moves.

I'd play exf7+. Your instinct to attack the king was right, but Qh5 rushed the queen out where it could get chased around; instead, use that strong pawn on e6 first. After exf7+ the rook recaptures on f7, and then you can follow up with Bxh7+ to strip open the king before bringing your queen into the attack. The key idea is to open lines and remove defenders *before* committing your queen, so it arrives with real threats instead of getting kicked.

Takeaway: Attack with your pawns and minor pieces first to open the king's cover, then bring the queen in for the finish.

I'd play R3f2. Your capture gxh6 was perfectly fine and lost nothing, but here's a chance to fix something more important: your rook on f3 was doing little while Black's rook sat happily on d2. By playing R3f2 you attack that intruding rook and force it to retreat or trade, taking away Black's active piece and keeping your own rooks connected and busy. In endgames like this, active rooks matter more than grabbing a pawn, so challenging his best-placed piece is the winning idea.

Takeaway: In rook endgames, kick your opponent's active rook back before hunting pawns.

I'd play g6. Your capture on h6 wasn't a mistake at all — it stays winning — but pushing g6 is the sharper, more instructive idea because it cramps Black and creates a passed pawn threat on the kingside. After g6 you hit the f7-pawn, and if Black takes with fxg6 you recapture hxg6, leaving a dangerous connected passer supported by your king and rooks. The lesson is that in these endings your advanced pawns are the real weapon, and pushing them tends to be stronger than trading them off.

Takeaway: In endgames, use extra pawns to advance and create passers rather than trading them away.

I'd play Bxh7+. Your instinct to attack the king was right, but Qh5 rushed the queen in before clearing a path — the sacrifice is what actually breaks the door down. Play Bxh7+ first: after Kxh7, then Qh5+ arrives with the king already dragged out and your pawns on e6 and g5 crowding the defense, so the attack has real force instead of just a lone queen probing. The key correction is sequencing — the classic Greek gift works because the bishop check pulls the king onto an exposed square before the queen commits.

Takeaway: In a kingside attack, sacrifice to open the king first, then bring the queen in with check — order matters.

I'd play exf7+. Your instinct to attack the king was right, but Qh5 rushed in before opening lines — the queen alone can't break through and just gets pushed around. Instead, use your advanced pawn: exf7+ forces a recapture (Rxf7), and once the f7 square is cleared and the king is loosened, Bxh7+ crashes into the king with real force. The key idea is to open the position around the king *before* committing your queen, so the attack lands with support instead of arriving alone.

Takeaway: Before throwing your queen at the enemy king, first open lines and bring force with pawns and minor pieces so the attack is backed up.

I'd play g6. Your gxh6 is completely fine and loses nothing, but g6 is the more instructive way to handle this passed-pawn race: instead of trading pawns and letting Black recapture, you fix the structure and cramp his kingside. After g6 fxg6 hxg6 your g-pawn is a monster that Black must permanently watch, and your rooks can support its advance while your king strolls forward. The key idea you missed is that pushing past a defender is often stronger than capturing it — you keep the pawn as a lasting asset rather than simplifying the tension away.

Takeaway: In pawn races, pushing a passer past the enemy's defenders often creates a more dangerous, permanent threat than trading it off.

I'd play Qh7. Your move h4 left the h3-pawn hanging and gave Black a free tempo to improve, so instead centralize your queen's activity: Qh7 hits both f7 and g6 while still covering h3, keeping pressure on Black's fragile pawns. Even if Black grabs f4, you retain a strong, active queen and coordinated play rather than a passive pawn push that solves nothing. The key is that in this open queen-and-pawn ending, keeping your queen doing multiple jobs at once beats making a slow, purposeless pawn move.

Takeaway: When your position is thin on material, put your queen where it attacks and defends at the same time instead of drifting with pawn moves.

I'd play Ng3. Grabbing on h6 with gxh6 felt natural, but after Black recaptures with the pawn you've just traded off your one advanced pawn and opened lines toward your own position without any lasting gain. Instead, finish getting your pieces into the game: Ng3 develops your last minor piece while also defending your bishop on e2 and your pawn on e4, so everything is solid and connected before you go looking for action. Once your knight is out you can bring your rook to a useful file and play from a stable, coordinated setup.

Takeaway: When there's no clear reward, develop and connect your pieces before rushing to grab a pawn.

I'd play Nxf3+. Your knight on h2 and your bishop on g4 are both a bit stranded, and the bishop on g4 is actually hanging — after your Qd7, White can simply grab it with fxg4. Instead, Nxf3+ gives check, wins the pawn, and forces White to respond right away; if they take with gxf3, you've traded off your loose knight and can then play Qd7 with a much healthier position. Takeaway: Before making a quiet move, check whether one of your own pieces is hanging and whether a check or capture can fix it first.
